title: Add cardinality guardrails for telemetry dimensions used by unit economics
preview:
Problem
Our unit economics pipeline relies on a small set of stable dimensions (model/version, region, customer_tier, streaming, quantization_profile, route). Recent dogfooding and prototype work showed multiple paths that could accidentally introduce runaway tag cardinality and degrade metrics/warehouse systems:
- quantization_profile values are currently free-form in some code paths (e.g., experiment IDs / git SHAs leaking into request metadata)
- route_id is sometimes emitted as an internal per-request or per-deploy identifier rather than a bounded route template
- customer identifiers occasionally show up in logs/metrics tags (especially via "debug" tags) and would explode cardinality + create privacy risk

This ticket implements hard guardrails so dimensions used by economics aggregation + dashboard queries stay bounded and safe.

Goals
- Prevent high-cardinality label explosions in metrics ingestion and downstream aggregation (Prometheus/Grafana + warehouse jobs)
- Ensure the economics metric stays queryable and reliable under load (no silent drops due to tag limits)
- Enforce privacy constraints: never emit raw customer_id as a telemetry dimension in production metering

Non-goals
- Redesigning the entire metering schema (use existing dimension set)
- Making every telemetry dimension low-cardinality everywhere; focus on the dimensions consumed by the unit economics pipeline

References
- Confluence: token-metering-dimensions-and-cardinality (standards)
- Linear: ENG-8425 (quantization_profile tagging), ENG-8426 (aggregation job)
- Related discussions: quant_profile allowed values + preventing cardinality explosions; top-N tables privacy review

Proposed approach
1) Centralize dimension validation in metering library
- Add a shared validation/normalization layer used by gateway + runtime emitters (single point of enforcement)
- For each guarded dimension, define: allowlist/regex, max length, and fallback behavior

2) quantization_profile
- Allowed values: bounded set sourced from model metadata owner (Quentin) and applied-ml conventions (e.g., fp16, int8, nf4, awq-int4, gptq-int4, "none")
- Reject/normalize anything else to "unknown" (or "other") and increment a counter: metering_dimension_dropped_total{dimension="quantization_profile",reason="invalid_value"}
- Hard cap length (e.g., 64 chars) to avoid experiment strings

3) route dimension
- Replace any per-request/unique IDs with a stable route template identifier (e.g., /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings)
- Add a max distinct values guard in-process (LRU of seen values per hour) and if exceeded, map additional values to "other" and emit a "route_overflow" counter
- Ensure tool-calling subroutes don’t generate per-tool names as route labels

4) customer identifiers
- Explicitly strip/disallow customer_id, org_id, project_id as metering dimensions in production
- If needed for debugging, route to structured logs with sampling (not metrics tags), behind internal-only flag; do not feed aggregation job

5) Monitoring & tests
- Add alerts/dashboards on dropped/normalized dimension counts (spike indicates regression)
- Add unit tests for:
  - quant_profile allowlist behavior
  - route template mapping
  - ensuring customer_id cannot be added as a dimension
- Add canary check coverage via ENG-8437 (output token metering regression tests) to include a dimension-safety assertion

Rollout plan
- Ship guardrails behind a config flag (metering.dimension_guardrails_enabled)
- Enable in staging + perf canary first; verify:
  - no major increase in "unknown" values
  - dashboard aggregates unchanged within tolerance (except where bad values previously existed)
- Gradually enable for production hosted first, then dedicated/private (dedicated tends to have more bespoke routing)
- Communicate to Applied ML / Runtime: quant profile strings must conform to allowlist; experiments should use separate fields/logging

Risks / trade-offs
- Normalizing to "unknown" can hide legitimately new values if we forget to update the allowlist; mitigate with dropped counters + weekly review
- Route mapping could reduce granularity for diagnosing certain regressions; mitigate via logs/tracing IDs rather than metrics labels

Acceptance criteria
- quantization_profile emitted by metering is bounded to the approved set; invalid values are normalized and counted
- route dimension is stable and does not include deploy/build/request-specific identifiers
- customer_id/org_id/project_id are not present as telemetry dimensions in production metering payloads
- A dashboard/alert exists for dropped/normalized dimensions with a documented oncall response
- Unit tests added to prevent regressions; CI fails if high-cardinality dimensions are introduced

Implementation notes (expected touch points)
- metering library: DimensionValidator + allowlist config
- gateway/runtime: replace direct tag assignment with validator output
- observability pack: add panels for metering_dimension_dropped_total + distinct label count sampling
- docs: update the standards page to reflect new enforcement behavior and how to onboard new quant profiles

Definition of done
- Guardrails enabled in production for hosted + dedicated and confirmed no metrics ingestion degradation
- SRE sign-off on alerts (noise tuned) and runbook snippet added to unit economics oncall guide

title: Backfill 2025 Q1 unit economics aggregates for Finance QTD reporting
preview:
Finance needs quarter-to-date (QTD) reporting for FY2026 KPI pack and signoff on blended $/1M output tokens. Current hourly aggregation job provides forward coverage, but Finance requires historical coverage for 2025 Q1 (2025-01-01 through 2025-03-31) so the dashboard can compare trends and reconcile against billing/infra spend.

Scope
- Add an idempotent backfill pathway for the unit economics hourly aggregation job to populate historical partitions for 2025 Q1.
- Ensure backfill respects the same dimension normalization and inclusion rules as the live pipeline (model identifier normalization, region normalization, customer tier derivation, quantization_profile allowed values).
- Provide safe operational controls (throttling / concurrency limits) so we do not overload the warehouse.
- Deliver verification queries + a lightweight reconciliation checklist for Finance/FP&A.

Non-goals
- Backfilling beyond 2025 Q1 in v1 (we can extend once Finance signs off on methodology).
- Perfect matching to customer billing at the invoice line level (handled via reconciliation report workstream).

Implementation notes / requirements
- Idempotency: rerunning any hour/day must not double-count. Use deterministic partition overwrite ("insert overwrite" semantics) keyed on hour + dimensions.
- Partitioning: backfill should write into the same partition scheme as live tables (hourly partitions; avoid full-table rewrites).
- Rate card versioning: use the approved rate card snapshot for the period being backfilled. For 2025 Q1, pin to the "2025Q4" rate card unless Finance approves a Q1-specific retro adjustment. Document which rate card version was applied in job metadata.
- Timezone/day boundary: Finance reporting uses America/Los_Angeles day boundaries for QTD rollups; ensure the backfill run window aligns with the canonical "reporting_day" transform used in views.
- Warehouse safety: default to low concurrency (e.g., 2-4 hours in parallel) and include a "dry run" mode that only computes row counts by partition.

Deliverables
1) Backfill command (CLI/job args)
- start_ts / end_ts (inclusive/exclusive specified in docs)
- max_parallel_partitions
- max_warehouse_slots (or equivalent)
- dry_run=true|false
- force_overwrite=true|false (guarded)

2) Runbook snippet (for oncall + Finance support)
- How to run the backfill for a fixed time window
- How to validate completeness (hour coverage, missing dimension thresholds)
- How to verify output token totals match metering aggregates within expected tolerance

3) Verification + signoff checklist
- Row count parity vs expected hours
- Token totals parity vs metering source
- Spot-check: top 5 models and top 3 regions for a selected week
- Reconciliation view deltas reviewed with FP&A

Acceptance criteria
- Backfill successfully populates all hourly partitions for 2025-01-01 00:00 through 2025-03-31 23:00 (reporting timezone transform documented).
- Re-running backfill for the same window produces identical results (no duplication/drift).
- Warehouse impact stays within agreed limits (no sustained query timeouts; no elevated latency for dashboard queries beyond SLO during backfill windows).
- Finance can run QTD reporting in dashboard and reconciliation report shows deltas within agreed tolerance, with known caveats documented.

Links
- PR (backfill tooling): sources/github/redwood/pr-19412-backfill-tooling-for-2025q1-economics.json
- Related: ENG-8426 (hourly aggregation job), ENG-8431 (view perf), ENG-8430 (reconciliation)
- Slack context: backfill questions + warehouse load (eng-oncall / eng-sre threads)

Risks / open questions
- Warehouse capacity: backfill may compete with peak dashboard usage; propose maintenance window runs (00:00-06:00 PT) with throttling.
- Late-arriving telemetry: decide whether to backfill from raw metering only once, or support a second "late data" sweep for a 7-day lag window.
- Rate card: confirm with Rishi whether to apply today’s rate card snapshot retroactively or use a pinned snapshot for historical stability.

Owner(s)
- Mei Lin (Data Eng) primary
- Caleb Johnson (DQ) to provide completeness checks
- Rishi Malhotra (FP&A) signoff on rate card/versioning and reporting boundaries

title: Region normalization: canonical mapping table + ingestion updates for unit economics grouping
preview:
Problem
Today we have multiple region identifiers across systems (gateway request metadata, runtime metrics tags, infra billing exports, and warehouse dimensions). The same physical region can be represented as:
- Cloud provider region (e.g., "us-east-1", "eu-west-1")
- Cloud friendly alias (e.g., "us-east", "eu-west")
- Internal colo labels (e.g., "sjc-01", "fra-02")
- Provider + region composites (e.g., "aws/us-east-1")

This is causing:
- Aggregation splits (same region appears as multiple rows)
- Dropped rows when joins to cost inputs fail
- Confusing drilldowns in Unit Economics dashboard (Finance sees inconsistent totals by region)

Goal
Introduce a canonical region mapping that:
1) Produces a stable, low-cardinality canonical_region dimension for analytics.
2) Preserves raw source region for debugging/audit.
3) Makes joins to GPU cost rate card / infra spend deterministic.
4) Has explicit behavior for unknown/unmapped regions and does not silently drop spend or tokens.

Scope
A) Data model: canonical mapping table + conventions
- Create a canonical region mapping table in the warehouse (authoritative for analytics):
  - raw_region (as observed)
  - raw_region_source (gateway|runtime|billing|infra_export)
  - provider (aws|gcp|azure|oci|internal)
  - canonical_region (e.g., "us-east", "us-west", "eu-west", "eu-central", "ap-southeast")
  - canonical_region_detail (optional; e.g., "us-east-1" where we need that granularity later)
  - is_active (for deprecations)
  - effective_start_ts / effective_end_ts (so historical data can be interpreted correctly)
  - notes (edge case rationale)

B) Ingestion changes
- Update metering/analytics ingestion to attach canonical_region to events used by unit economics rollups.
- Ensure we keep raw_region for debugging and can recompute canonical mapping if table changes.

C) Backfill + validation
- Backfill last 30d of hourly aggregates (or underlying fact table if required) to ensure unit economics dashboard is consistent for Finance dogfood.
- Add validation queries + data quality checks:
  - % of tokens where canonical_region is null/unknown per hour
  - token share by canonical_region over time for sudden shifts (catch mapping regressions)

Non-goals
- Changing customer-visible region semantics.
- Introducing per-AZ or per-colo canonicalization in v1 (we’ll keep a coarse canonical_region for the dashboard).

Proposed canonical_region values (v1)
- us-east
- us-west
- eu-west
- eu-central
- ap-southeast
- ap-northeast
- ap-south
- sa-east
- me-central
- unknown

Mapping rules (v1)
- If raw region is a known cloud region ("us-east-1", "us-east-2", "us-west-2", "eu-west-1", "eu-central-1", etc.), map to the corresponding canonical.
- If raw region is already a canonical alias ("us-east", "eu-west"), keep.
- If raw region is an internal colo ("sjc-01", "fra-02"), map using internal->canonical lookup maintained by Infra (initial seed from infra billing exports).
- For malformed/empty strings: canonical_region = "unknown" and retain raw_region for debugging.

Acceptance criteria
- Warehouse has an authoritative mapping table with documented ownership and update process.
- Unit economics aggregation outputs (hourly) have canonical_region populated for >= 99.5% of rows (token-weighted) for Hosted + Dedicated.
- No silent row drops due to failed joins to cost inputs; unknown regions show up explicitly as "unknown".
- Finance can run region drilldowns without seeing obvious alias duplication (e.g., "eu-west" and "eu-west-1" as separate rows).

Implementation notes
- Prefer mapping at ingestion time for dashboard performance and to avoid repeated joins in query paths.
- Keep raw_region + raw_region_source for auditability and mapping corrections.
- If we need canonical_region_detail later, do NOT change canonical_region semantics; add a new dimension.

References
- Region normalization edge cases thread: sources/slack/eng-platform/1737510088-region-normalization-edge-cases.json
- Plan draft: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/region-normalization-plan.json

Rollout plan
1) Land mapping table + initial seed values.
2) Update ingestion to compute canonical_region.
3) Run in shadow mode for 48h: compare region totals pre/post mapping (should mostly merge, not change token totals).
4) Enable in unit economics rollups + dashboard APIs.
5) Backfill 2025-QTD window once stable; coordinate warehouse load with SRE.

Owner
- Primary: Mei Lin
- Review: Nadia Rahman (metering dimensions), Cole Summers (cost attribution joins)

Risks / open questions
- How to handle multi-region internal colo naming where a colo label doesn’t clearly map to a cloud region (needs Infra signoff).
- Whether Dedicated private regions should map to the same canonical bucket or a separate category (v1: same canonical bucket, tier will separate).

title: Build hourly unit economics aggregation job (cost per output token by model/region/tier)
preview:
Goal
Build the hourly aggregation job that produces finance-aligned unit economics tables/views for the internal “Unit Economics” dashboard. Primary KPI is blended $/1M output tokens with drilldowns by model(+version/+quant profile), region, and customer tier.

Context
We currently have (a) request/token metering and (b) infra spend inputs (GPU rate card + capacity metadata), but no consistent hourly warehouse layer that joins + allocates costs and produces a stable set of dimensions for dashboard queries. Finance requires a definition that reconciles to spend inputs with documented allocation rules and versioning.

Deliverables (v1)
1) Hourly aggregation job (scheduled) that:
- Reads metering facts (output tokens at minimum; optionally input tokens for additional analysis) at an hourly grain.
- Applies canonical dimension normalization (model identifiers, region mapping, customer tier derivation, streaming flag, quantization profile).
- Allocates costs from the hourly cost inputs to the same grain using the cost attribution model defined in ADR-034.
- Writes results to warehouse tables and publishes stable views for the dashboard query API.

2) Backfill + idempotency
- Job supports re-running a given hour range without duplicating or drifting results.
- Backfill tool can populate historical hours for 2025Q1 at minimum (Finance QTD reporting requirement).

3) Operational hooks
- Basic job metrics (rows processed, hours covered, late/missing hours) and failure alerts to unit-econ oncall rotation.
- Guardrails to prevent runaway cardinality (quantization_profile and any route identifiers).

Warehouse outputs (proposed)
- analytics.unit_econ_hourly_fact
  - Grain: hour_ts, model_key, model_version, quantization_profile, region, customer_tier, streaming_flag
  - Measures: output_tokens, request_count, blended_cost_usd, blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens
  - Optional measures (if available by then): cache_hit_rate, batching_utilization, avg_output_len_tokens, prefill_decode_ratio

- analytics.unit_econ_hourly_rollup (materialized)
  - Same measures rolled up to common dashboard groupings:
    - by hour, model_key
    - by hour, region
    - by hour, customer_tier
    - by hour, model_key + region + tier

- analytics.v_unit_econ_hourly_dashboard (stable view)
  - Normalized columns + computed fields used by Console query endpoints.

Idempotency design
- Use MERGE/UPSERT keyed by (hour_ts, model_key, model_version, quantization_profile, region, customer_tier, streaming_flag).
- Ensure cost inputs are versioned (rate_card_version, allocation_model_version) so reruns are deterministic; store both versions on each row.
- If upstream dimension mappings change (e.g., region normalization), rerun with an explicit “recompute” flag for affected hours.

Backfill design
- CLI/runner: unit-econ-backfill --start=YYYY-MM-DDTHH --end=YYYY-MM-DDTHH --max-hours-per-batch=N
- Throttle/concurrency controls to avoid warehouse timeouts (SRE guidance after earlier backfill attempts).
- Verification query printed at end of each batch (hours inserted, distinct dims, total output tokens).

Open questions
- How to treat “unknown” region/tier during early rollout: keep rows with region='unknown' and tier='unknown' (preferred) vs dropping (Finance prefers completeness with a confidence indicator).
- Whether to include idle allocation directly in blended_cost_usd at the hourly grain, or compute as a separate component for transparency (leans toward separate columns: direct_cost_usd + idle_alloc_usd + overhead_alloc_usd).

Notes
This ticket is intentionally scoped to the aggregation job + warehouse layer. API endpoints/UI are handled in ENG-8427/ENG-8428. Data quality checks/alerts are covered by ENG-8429 but we should emit basic job metrics needed for those checks.

title: Verify cache + batching signals correlate with blended cost in unit economics model; identify instrumentation gaps
preview:
Context / why this exists
The Unit Economics dashboard is surfacing blended $/1M output tokens as the primary KPI. Product/Eng want drilldowns and contributing signals (cache hit rate, batching efficiency, output length, streaming vs non-streaming, quant profile) that actually explain cost movements. Finance is aligned on the cost attribution methodology, but the dashboard will be misleading if the runtime “explanatory” signals don’t correlate with blended cost in the expected direction, or if we’re missing instrumentation on high-volume code paths.

This ticket validates that:
1) Our caching metrics (KV/prefix cache) correlate with lower blended $/1M output tokens after controlling for major confounders.
2) Our batching metrics correlate with lower blended $/1M output tokens (or higher tokens/GPU-hour) in hosted/dedicated contexts where batching applies.
3) We can trust these signals (coverage + stability + dimension correctness). If not, we identify what’s missing and propose concrete instrumentation changes.

Scope
In-scope
- Quantitative correlation checks (directionality + magnitude + lag) between runtime signals and unit cost.
- Coverage checks: % of traffic with non-null signals by route/model/region/tier and by response mode (streaming vs non-streaming).
- Identify missing instrumentation and propose follow-up runtime/gateway telemetry additions.
- Update the data dictionary notes for how to interpret the signals in the dashboard (and when not to).

Out of scope
- Implementing runtime instrumentation changes (will spin follow-up ENG tickets once gaps are confirmed).
- Designing dashboard UX (handled in DES tickets / Console work).

Hypotheses to validate
H1: Higher prefix/KV cache hit rate => lower blended $/1M output tokens (and/or higher tokens/GPU-hour), especially for long-context workloads.
H2: Higher batching utilization (effective batch size / active decode lanes) => lower blended $/1M output tokens for hosted + dedicated pools.
H3: Signal changes should precede cost changes with a short lag (same hour or next hour, depending on aggregation window).

Important confounders / controls
We need to control or stratify by:
- model + model version + quantization_profile
- region (normalized)
- customer tier (self-serve / dedicated / private / enterprise)
- streaming flag (metering parity work is ongoing; don’t conclude anything if streaming coverage is incomplete)
- average output length (output tokens/request) and prefill/decode ratio (if available)
- traffic mix shifts (top routes) and time-of-day effects

Signals under test (current expected inputs)
From runtime / metering dimensions:
- kv_cache_hit_rate (or equivalent)
- prefix_cache_hit_rate (if distinct)
- cache_savings_tokens (if present) OR cacheable_prefill_tokens (preferred)
- batching_utilization (definition tbd)
- effective_batch_size (preferred)
- concurrent_decode_slots_used / max_slots (proxy)

From unit economics tables:
- blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens
- output_tokens
- gpu_cost_allocated (or “compute_cost_allocated” depending on attribution model)

Open issue: naming/availability of these metrics varies by code path. Part of this ticket is to document what’s actually populated and where.

Method / analysis plan
1) Data quality + coverage checks (must pass before correlation work)
- For the last 14 days, compute per-hour coverage:
  - % of requests with cache metric present
  - % of requests with batching metric present
  - % of output tokens attributable to rows with these metrics present
- Slice by:
  - streaming vs non-streaming
  - model, region, tier
  - top 20 routes by token volume
- Flag any dimension where coverage < 95% of output tokens (hosted) or any sustained gaps > 2 hours.

2) Correlation checks (directionality + robustness)
- Start with per-hour aggregates grouped by (model, region, tier, quant_profile, streaming_flag) to reduce mix issues.
- Compute:
  - Spearman correlation between cache_hit_rate and blended $/1M output tokens
  - Spearman correlation between effective_batch_size (or batching utilization) and blended $/1M output tokens
  - Simple regression / partial correlation controlling for output_tokens/request and traffic volume (only if needed; keep interpretation simple for v1)
- Evaluate correlations at:
  - same-hour
  - t+1 hour (lag)

3) Sanity checks to avoid false conclusions
- Verify cost attribution math: if blended cost includes idle allocation, then batching improvements may not show up strongly in blended $/token during low-utilization windows (idle dominates). Stratify by utilization regime if we have pool utilization.
- Dedicated/private: batching may be constrained or irrelevant depending on isolation settings; interpret separately.
- Ensure we’re not correlating a metric with itself via derived fields (e.g., if cost model uses the same runtime metric directly).

4) Deliverables
- A short write-up (1–2 pages) summarizing:
  - what correlates reliably
  - where signals are missing or unstable
  - recommended next instrumentation (what + where + owner)
  - recommended dashboard caveats (tooltip text / confidence indicator inputs)
- A set of “golden” verification queries (warehouse) for oncall investigations.

Proposed acceptance criteria
- Coverage:
  - For hosted traffic (self-serve + enterprise hosted), >= 98% of output tokens have non-null cache signal and non-null batching signal (or a documented reason why not applicable).
  - Streaming: parity confirmed post ENG-8421; until then, streaming rows are explicitly excluded from any correlation claims.
- Correlation directionality:
  - For at least 3 high-volume models across 2 regions, cache hit rate shows negative correlation with blended $/1M output tokens (or positive correlation with tokens/GPU-hour) that’s consistent week-over-week.
  - For batching, the relationship is consistent in hosted pools during high-util windows; if idle cost dominates, document that limitation explicitly.
- Gaps:
  - Any missing instrumentation is captured as follow-up issues with concrete field definitions, cardinality guidance, and target rollout.

Notes on current risks / known issues
- Streaming output token undercount historically made blended $/token noisy; do not interpret correlations until post-fix window (ENG-8421 / PR-19355).
- Quantization profile tagging is still rolling out; correlation needs to pin quant_profile where possible to avoid mixing.
- Cache hit rate instrumentation quality has been debated (e.g., what counts as a “hit” on partial prefill). We should validate the definition against runtime logs for at least one model.

Implementation notes (what I’ve done so far)
- Pulled last 7 days of hourly aggregates from the unit economics views and joined to runtime signal aggregates by (ts_hour, model_id, region, tier, streaming_flag).
- Early read: cache metrics are populated for most non-streaming hosted traffic, but batching utilization is missing/null on a meaningful fraction of decode-heavy routes (suspect gateway path not emitting the batch stats dimension consistently).
- Observed a weak-to-moderate negative correlation between cache_hit_rate and blended $/1M output tokens on long-context workloads, but it breaks down in hours where total GPU cost is dominated by idle allocation (consistent with ADR-034 treatment). This likely needs a dashboard caveat: blended cost is not purely marginal.

Instrumentation gaps to confirm (likely follow-ups)
- Missing “effective_batch_size” (or a stable batching utilization definition) on:
  - streaming decode path
  - tool-calling / function calling response path
  - some multi-tenant gateway routes (suspected older runtime client)
- Need “prefill_tokens” and “decode_tokens” split per request (or per batch) to compute prefill/decode ratio reliably and explain cost spikes for long-context prompts.
- Cache metrics ambiguous across cache types; propose explicitly:
  - prefix_cache_hit_rate (prefill)
  - kv_cache_reuse_rate (decode continuation) OR “prefill_cacheable_tokens” + “prefill_cache_hits_tokens”

Coordination / owners
- Telemetry / aggregation: Logan Wright, Mei Lin
- Token metering correctness: Nadia Rahman
- Runtime metrics definitions: (loop in runtime team via Sean Gallagher for escalation; primary runtime contact tbd)
- Finance methodology caveats: Rishi Malhotra

Update log
2025-02-07 (Caleb): Started coverage report. Non-streaming looks mostly complete; streaming coverage still inconsistent (expected; waiting on fix rollout confirmation).
2025-02-11 (Caleb): Joined unit econ hourly aggregates to runtime signal aggregates. Found batching signal nulls concentrated in a small number of high-token routes; suspect instrumentation mismatch between runtime client versions. Drafted list of top affected routes/models.
2025-02-14 (Nadia): Confirmed streaming metering fix is in canary; advised re-running analysis starting 48h after full rollout to avoid partial data.
2025-02-18 (Caleb): Preliminary correlation: cache_hit_rate correlates with lower blended $/token for long-context models in us-east + eu-west when stratifying by quant_profile; batching correlation unclear in low-utilization hours (idle dominates). Next step: stratify by utilization regime (need pool utilization metric or proxy) and validate batching metric definition with runtime team.

Next steps
- Re-run coverage + correlation for 14-day window excluding partial streaming rollout days.
- Add stratification by utilization regime (ask Cole Summers / infra cost attribution if we have hourly “active GPU hours vs allocated GPU hours” by pool).
- Schedule 30-min review with Nadia + Mei Lin to agree on which batching/cache fields are authoritative for the dashboard.
- Draft follow-up ENG tickets for missing instrumentation with concrete field schemas + cardinality limits (align with token-metering-dimensions-and-cardinality standard).

How to review
- Looking for: agreement on signal definitions, whether the observed relationships make sense given cost attribution (including idle), and confirmation on which gaps are highest priority for v1 vs v1.1.

title: Add quantization_profile dimension to token metering (with cardinality guardrails)
preview:
Problem
- Unit Economics v1 needs drilldowns by quantization profile (e.g., fp16 vs int8 vs int4) to attribute cost improvements/regressions correctly.
- Today metering does not include a stable, low-cardinality quantization identifier. ML/Runtime experiments are tracked ad-hoc, and Finance/Optimize can’t reliably segment $/1M output tokens by quant profile.
- Risk: introducing a free-form tag will explode telemetry cardinality (and break aggregation, M3/Grafana, warehouse costs).

Goal
- Add a new dimension `quantization_profile` to token metering events used by the Unit Economics pipeline.
- Enforce a strict allowlist / normalization so the dimension stays low-cardinality and safe for production metrics.

Non-goals
- Encoding detailed per-layer quant params (scales/zeros) in telemetry.
- Backfilling historical raw events; unit economics backfill will treat missing profile as `unknown`.

Proposed dimension semantics
- Field name: `quantization_profile`
- Type: string
- Allowed values (v1 allowlist):
  - `fp16`
  - `bf16`
  - `int8_w8a16`
  - `int4_awq`
  - `int4_gptq`
  - `int4_w4a16` (only if we actually run this profile in prod; otherwise omit)
  - `unknown` (explicit default)
- Source of truth: serving stack/model metadata (not user input). If the request uses a compiled artifact/engine with a known profile, emit it. Otherwise emit `unknown`.

Implementation plan (high level)
1) Emit profile from serving stack
- Runtime/gateway path should attach quant profile to request metadata at completion time (same place we attach model_id/model_version).
- IMPORTANT: do not allow callers/SDKs to set this dimension directly.

2) Metering schema update
- Add `quantization_profile` to the metering event schema(s) consumed by the unit economics aggregation job.
- Ensure it is included in both streaming and non-streaming completion events (parity requirement for unit economics).

3) Cardinality guardrails
- Enforce allowlist at the emission point (preferred) and again in ingestion (defense in depth).
- Normalization rules:
  - trim/ lowercase
  - map common aliases (e.g., `awq_int4` -> `int4_awq`, `gptq-int4` -> `int4_gptq`)
  - everything else -> `unknown`
- Add a "distinct values" monitor for `quantization_profile` per day per environment; page only if exceeds threshold.
- Threshold proposal: <= 25 distinct values/day in prod; warn at 10 in staging.

4) Data model integration
- Update unit economics aggregation/group-by dimensions to include `quantization_profile` (behind feature flag until data coverage is acceptable).
- Update data dictionary to document meaning + interpretation notes (e.g., profile indicates serving quantization, not training-time quant).

Acceptance criteria
- `quantization_profile` present on >= 99.5% of completed metering events for supported models (remaining are `unknown`).
- Distinct `quantization_profile` values in prod <= 25/day and stable week-over-week.
- No increase in metrics/warehouse cost attributable to tag explosion.
- Unit economics drilldown by quant profile works for at least one high-traffic model in two regions.

Testing
- Unit tests for normalization/allowlist mapping.
- Integration test to ensure the dimension is present on both streaming and non-streaming completion paths.
- Canary validation: compare distribution of `quantization_profile` vs known deployed engines for a target model.

Rollout
- Stage: emit field + validate distinct values + verify ingestion.
- Prod: enable emission for a small allowlisted model set first; expand once cardinality monitors are green.
- After 48h stable: enable aggregation group-by support for internal dashboard.

References
- Telemetry dimension cardinality standard: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/token-metering-dimensions-and-cardinality.json
- Quant profile attribution notes: sources/confluence/applied-ml-and-evals/quantization-profiles/quantization-profile-cost-impact-tracking.json
- Related project: Internal Unit Economics Dashboard (blended $/1M output tokens)

title: Customer tier dimension derivation (hosted/dedicated/private/enterprise) + backfill
preview:
## Context
Unit Economics dashboard requires a canonical `customer_tier` dimension for aggregation and Finance reporting. Today we have multiple inconsistent signals (billing plan, contract flags, routing/dedicated pools, private deployments) and historical data lacks a stable tier value.

This ticket implements:
1) tier derivation logic in the telemetry pipeline (so new events have `customer_tier`), and
2) backfill for historical events/aggregates (so Finance can report QTD + historical comparisons).

Primary use: drilldown and comparison in blended $/1M output tokens by tier: `self_serve`, `dedicated`, `private`, `enterprise`.

## Proposed tier taxonomy (v1)
Canonical values (lowercase snake_case):
- `self_serve`: hosted API usage paid via self-serve plan (including free credits, trials) and not covered by enterprise contract.
- `enterprise`: hosted API usage covered by an enterprise contract / negotiated terms (even if technically using shared hosted fleet).
- `dedicated`: usage served from dedicated capacity (reserved GPU pools) regardless of billing plan.
- `private`: usage served from a customer-controlled private deployment (VPC/on-prem).

Notes:
- This is a reporting dimension, not a product entitlement. Do not use this to gate features.
- Mutually exclusive: exactly one tier per request/event.

## Source of truth + precedence rules
We need deterministic precedence to avoid double counting or flapping.

### Inputs
- `request_context.deployment_mode` (runtime/gateway): hosted | dedicated | private
- `request_context.dedicated_pool_id` (nullable)
- `request_context.private_deployment_id` (nullable)
- Billing system snapshot: `billing_accounts.plan_type` (self_serve | enterprise), `contracts.contract_type`, `contracts.effective_start/end`
- Contract metadata flags: `is_reseller`, `reseller_parent_account_id`, `pilot_program`, `credits_only`

### Precedence
1) If `private_deployment_id` present OR `deployment_mode == private` => `private`
2) Else if `dedicated_pool_id` present OR `deployment_mode == dedicated` => `dedicated`
3) Else if billing/contract snapshot indicates enterprise coverage at request time => `enterprise`
4) Else => `self_serve`

### Temporal correctness
Tier must be computed as-of the request timestamp. We cannot join “current tier” for historical events.
Plan:
- Build/maintain a `customer_tier_history` table keyed by `billing_account_id` with effective date ranges (start_ts, end_ts) derived from contract/billing change events.
- Derivation job joins request events on `billing_account_id` and `request_ts` to determine enterprise vs self_serve when in hosted mode.

## Edge cases (must handle)
### Resellers
If `is_reseller = true`:
- Requests should still be tiered by how they are served. If reseller routes through hosted shared fleet and is covered by an enterprise-like contract, tier should be `enterprise`.
- Do NOT attempt to “attribute” tier to the reseller’s end customer in v1. Avoid introducing `end_customer_id` dimension (privacy + cardinality risk).
- Ensure `billing_account_id` used for tier history is the billed entity (reseller parent) where applicable. If ambiguous, default to billed entity mapping and log.

### Pilots / credits-only / promo
- If served on hosted shared fleet and not under enterprise contract coverage: `self_serve`.
- If under enterprise contract but with $0 due to negotiated credits: `enterprise` (Finance wants tier aligned to contract, not invoice amount).
- Explicitly exclude internal dogfood accounts from aggregation via existing `is_internal` filters (separate mechanism). Tier derivation can still return a value for internal accounts.

### Dedicated burst / mixed serving
Some enterprise customers may burst from dedicated to hosted fallback during capacity events.
- Tier should reflect where it actually ran (dedicated vs hosted) using request context. If hosted fallback occurs and enterprise contract applies, tier becomes `enterprise` for those requests.
- This is expected and useful for diagnosing cost spikes during failover.

### Multi-contract / contract transition windows
- If overlapping enterprise contracts exist, treat enterprise coverage as true if ANY contract covers the request timestamp.
- If contract start/end boundaries are day-aligned in Finance systems, beware UTC vs local day boundary. Use UTC timestamps in pipeline; Finance reconciliation can roll up by Finance timezone in reporting layer.

### Unknown / missing signals
- If request context is missing (rare ingestion bug), fall back to billing snapshot: enterprise vs self_serve; else `self_serve`.
- Track an `unknown_tier_reason` counter internally (not a dimension) for data quality monitoring.

## Implementation plan
1) Telemetry ingestion: add `customer_tier` to the metering event schema (dimension, low cardinality).
   - Owner: Cole Summers (implementation) + Nadia Rahman (schema review).
   - Ensure it follows dimension standards (see token metering dimensions/cardinality guidelines).

2) Build tier derivation module
   - Library function: `derive_customer_tier(request_context, billing_account_id, request_ts)`.
   - Hosted tier resolution: query `customer_tier_history` (warehouse) for backfill; for streaming/near-real-time pipeline use cached tier snapshots updated hourly.

3) Backfill
   - Populate `customer_tier` for last 2 quarters of events (minimum: 2025-Q1 per Finance).
   - Strategy: backfill at the aggregation layer first (hourly unit econ aggregates) to unblock dashboard; optionally backfill raw events later.
   - Guardrails: throttle to avoid warehouse timeouts; schedule during low-usage window; idempotent partitions.

4) Data quality
   - Add completeness check: % of output tokens with non-null tier by hour >= 99.9% (threshold TBD with SRE).
   - Add drift check: distribution shift alert (e.g., `self_serve` share changes > X% WoW) to catch mapping regressions.

5) Rollout
   - Stage behind a feature flag in pipeline (`emit_customer_tier_dimension`).
   - Dogfood in internal dashboard with Finance before enabling globally.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All newly ingested metering events include `customer_tier` with allowed values only.
- [ ] Backfill covers 2025-Q1 onward for unit economics aggregates with tier breakdown.
- [ ] Unknown/missing tier rate < 0.1% of output tokens in a 7-day window.
- [ ] Finance validation: sample of 20 known accounts matches expected tier across contract transitions.
- [ ] Documentation updated (link from metric dictionary / oncall guide).

## Validation / test plan
- Unit tests for precedence ordering and edge cases (reseller, pilot credits, dedicated/private overrides).
- Integration test: pick 3 accounts (one self-serve, one enterprise hosted, one dedicated) and verify end-to-end (request -> metering -> warehouse view -> dashboard).
- Reconciliation check: totals across tiers sum to overall totals for a time window (no gaps).

## Links
- Drive: customer tier mapping notes: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/customer-tier-mapping-notes.json
- Confluence: metric definitions: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/unit-economics-dashboard-metrics-and-definitions.json
- ADR (cost attribution model context): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-034-unit-economics-cost-attribution-model.json
- Related eng tickets: ENG-8426 (hourly aggregation job), ENG-8430 (reconciliation vs billing)

## Open questions
1) Do we need a separate `enterprise_dedicated` tier? Proposal: no; keep tier based on actual serving mode + enterprise only for hosted, to preserve mutual exclusivity.
2) For resellers, do we always map to reseller parent billing account? Need confirmation from FP&A (Rishi) on reporting expectations.
3) Should we freeze tier history at month-close for Finance views? Likely handled in Finance reporting layer; not required for v1.

## Notes for reviewers
- Please sanity-check precedence rules against Dedicated/Private routing behavior.
- Finance reviewers: confirm contract-effective timestamp handling and reseller expectations.

title: Normalize model identifiers for unit economics aggregation (runtime/gateway/analytics)
preview:
Problem
Today the same model shows up under different identifiers across the stack, which breaks grouping for blended $/1M output tokens and makes reconciliation fragile.

Examples seen in staging/dogfood:
- Gateway request uses a user-facing alias (e.g., "llama-3.1-70b-instruct", "meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct", or "rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct")
- Runtime logs sometimes emit an internal engine string (e.g., "llama3_1_70b_instruct_fp8" or a build label)
- Analytics tables have a mix of alias, marketing name, and a partially-normalized slug
- Quantization profile is sometimes appended to the model name (e.g., "...-fp8"), which inflates cardinality and makes versioning ambiguous

Impact
- Unit economics aggregation groups the same underlying workload into multiple rows, hiding real regressions/outliers
- Finance reconciliation vs billing is noisy because reporting totals depend on which alias is selected
- Drilldowns by model version/quant profile are unreliable (and sometimes impossible)

Goal
Define and implement a single canonical model identity in telemetry and warehouse views so unit economics queries can group consistently by:
(1) model (canonical), (2) model version/build, and (3) quantization profile (separate dimension).

Non-goals (this ticket)
- Redesign of the entire model catalog UX in Console
- Changing public API model names/aliases (we will support aliases, but normalize downstream)

Proposed canonical fields (for economics + telemetry)
We will standardize on the following dimensions (names are WIP; the key is the semantics):
- model_id: Canonical, stable ID for the logical model offering (human-readable slug, but treated as an ID)
  - Examples: "llama-3.1-70b-instruct", "mistral-large-2", "qwen2.5-72b-instruct"
  - Must NOT include quantization, region, hardware, or engine-specific suffixes
- model_version: Redwood-controlled serving package version (changes when weights/config/tokenizer change)
  - Examples: "2025-01-22.1" (date-based), or semver if we already have it for the catalog
- model_alias: The string passed by the customer/gateway (optional; for debugging only, not for primary grouping)
- quantization_profile: Separate dimension (e.g., "fp16", "bf16", "fp8_e4m3", "int8_kv", etc.), with allowed-values guardrails

Source of truth
- The authoritative mapping from alias -> (model_id, model_version default, upstream_model_ref) will come from the Model Catalog/Registry owned by Quentin.
- Analytics/telemetry will consume a versioned snapshot of this mapping so backfills and Finance reporting are stable.

Implementation plan
1) Define canonicalization contract
- Write a short contract in the working data dictionary: required fields, allowed characters, versioning rules, and examples.
- Align with cardinality constraints in the token metering standard (model_id must be low/medium cardinality; model_version medium; alias potentially high but we will cap/redact).

2) Emit canonical identifiers at the source where possible
- Gateway: when resolving a request model name, attach resolved model_id and model_version to request metadata that flows into token metering.
- Runtime: ensure the runtime does not overwrite/replace canonical fields with engine strings; runtime may emit engine_id separately if needed.

3) Normalize at ingestion as a safety net
- Add an ingestion-time normalization step that maps legacy model strings to canonical model_id/model_version using a versioned mapping table.
- Unknown/unmapped values should map to model_id="unknown" and preserve raw value in a debug field (with sampling) to avoid dropping rows.

4) Backfill
- Backfill last 30 days of metering aggregates once mapping is in place so Finance can run QTD reporting without alias fragmentation.

5) Update downstream aggregation
- ENG-8426 aggregation job should group by canonical model_id and model_version, and treat quantization_profile as its own dimension.

Acceptance criteria
- For a fixed time window, sum(output_tokens) is invariant (within expected late-arrival tolerance) when switching between legacy and canonical grouping.
- Top-N models table no longer shows duplicate rows that differ only by alias/formatting.
- model_id cardinality remains within agreed guardrails; model_alias is either not stored, sampled, or explicitly excluded from high-volume aggregation tables.
- Reconciliation report (ENG-8430) can attribute deltas to either unmapped model_id="unknown" or known alias migrations, not silent fragmentation.

Open questions
- What is the exact definition of model_version for Dedicated/Private deployments where customers may pin images differently? Proposed: still emit a Redwood serving package version (image tag or bundle hash), but we need a stable representation.
- Do we need separate fields for "upstream_model" vs "redwood_served_model"? For v1, model_id is the served offering; upstream ref can live in the registry and be joined when needed.

Risks / gotchas
- Model fallback routing: requests may start on model A and fall back to model B. Telemetry must reflect the actual served model_id (not the requested alias).
- Shadow traffic / eval routes can pollute top-N if not labeled; ensure route_type or env is available for filtering.

Suggested reviewers
- Nadia Rahman (metering schema + streaming parity)
- Mei Lin (warehouse views + backfill)
- Chloe Martin / Felix Schneider (dashboard query expectations)

How to validate
- Run a one-day comparison query by (raw_model_string) vs (model_id/model_version) and ensure that rows collapse as expected.
- Spot check a handful of known models across regions and tiers.
- Verify unknown bucket stays <0.5% of tokens after rollout (threshold configurable).

title: Instrument canonical per-deployment throughput metrics (deployment_id/model/region) for Dedicated SLO
preview:
Context / why
We are formalizing a Dedicated throughput SLO and need a single canonical throughput signal that is attributable to a specific Dedicated deployment. Today we have a mix of per-pod / per-cluster perf counters and some request logs, but nothing that can be used as a stable SLO input for: (a) per-customer reporting (deployment_id), (b) tier-aware dashboards, and (c) SLO burn-rate alerts. This ticket adds per-deployment throughput metrics with consistent token accounting semantics (esp. streaming) and the required labels.

Scope
- Emit canonical throughput “inputs” at the inference gateway / request path for all Dedicated deployments:
  - tokens counted (input + output) with explicit definitions
  - wall clock generation time used for throughput calculations
  - request counts by completion outcome
- All metrics MUST be tagged by:
  - deployment_id (Dedicated deployment identifier; required)
  - model (canonical model slug as used in routing/catalog)
  - region (Redwood region for the serving pool)
- Provide dashboard hooks (metric names/labels/query examples) for Observability to add panels.
- Add acceptance tests and backfill documentation for metric semantics so SRE can write the SLO definition without re-litigating token accounting.

Non-goals
- This ticket does not define the final SLO thresholds or tier entitlements (handled in PM-482 / SRE SLO doc).
- This ticket does not build the full dashboards (handled by Observability). We provide stable metric contracts + sample queries.
- This ticket does not change routing behavior, admission control, or capacity policies.

Proposed metric contract (canonical)
Naming (Prometheus-style; final names can be adjusted to fit existing conventions as long as semantics match):
1) redwood_inference_generation_tokens_total
   - Type: counter
   - Value: number of tokens generated for responses (output tokens only)
   - Labels: deployment_id, model, region, streaming=(true|false)
   - Notes:
     - For streaming, increment tokens as they are flushed to the client.
     - For non-streaming, increment at request completion.

2) redwood_inference_prompt_tokens_total
   - Type: counter
   - Value: number of prompt tokens processed (input tokens)
   - Labels: deployment_id, model, region
   - Notes:
     - Includes system + user messages and any tool/function call arguments included in the prompt.

3) redwood_inference_generation_time_seconds_total
   - Type: counter
   - Value: wall-clock time attributable to token generation (seconds)
   - Labels: deployment_id, model, region, streaming=(true|false)
   - Definition:
     - Start: first token generation begins (post-queue, post-admission, after request assigned to a worker)
     - End: last generated token produced (or cancellation/timeout)
     - Excludes: time spent waiting in the front-door queue / admission control queue (we need a separate queue-time metric for saturation debugging, but SLO throughput should not be polluted by queue policy)

4) redwood_inference_requests_total
   - Type: counter
   - Value: number of completion/chat requests
   - Labels: deployment_id, model, region, status=(success|client_canceled|server_error|timeout|rate_limited|invalid_request)

Derived throughput (used by dashboards/SLO tooling)
- Output throughput (tokens/sec):
  sum(rate(redwood_inference_generation_tokens_total[5m])) / sum(rate(redwood_inference_generation_time_seconds_total[5m]))
- Optional “total tokens/sec” (prompt+output) for capacity planning:
  (sum(rate(redwood_inference_prompt_tokens_total[5m])) + sum(rate(redwood_inference_generation_tokens_total[5m]))) / sum(rate(redwood_inference_generation_time_seconds_total[5m]))

Rationale / semantics notes
- We will compute throughput from counters (tokens + time) instead of directly emitting tokens/sec as a gauge to avoid per-instance aggregation artifacts.
- We separate generation time from queue time so that overload protection changes do not retroactively change throughput measurement.
- We keep streaming=(true|false) to debug differences, but the canonical SLO query should aggregate both unless SRE decides otherwise.

Cardinality / performance constraints
- deployment_id is high cardinality but bounded by Dedicated footprint and required for customer attribution.
- model and region are low-ish cardinality.
- Do NOT add request_id or customer_id labels.
- Ensure metrics are emitted once per request per component (avoid double-counting across gateway + worker).

Implementation plan
1) Instrumentation location
- Emit counters in the request lifecycle code path where we already have:
  - resolved deployment_id (Dedicated only)
  - resolved model slug (post-routing)
  - region (serving pool region)
  - token counts from tokenizer + runtime
- For streaming, tokens are often counted at both “generated” and “sent” layers. We must choose one canonical point.
  - Decision: count output tokens at the flush-to-client boundary to reflect what the customer receives and to align with billing token accounting.

2) Token accounting sources
- Prompt tokens: from request normalization/tokenization stage.
- Output tokens: from streaming chunk encoder / final response serialization (single source of truth).
- Generation time: measured around the model execution phase (post-admission). Use monotonic clock.

3) Dedicated vs Hosted behavior
- Emit metrics only when deployment_id is present (Dedicated deployments). No-op for hosted multi-tenant routes for now to keep cardinality manageable.
- If we later need hosted per-route metrics, we’ll do that in a separate ticket with sampling and different labels.

4) Dashboard hooks
- Provide a short “how to query” section to Sergio/Allison for Grafana panels:
  - throughput floor line
  - throughput observed (p50/p95 across 5m windows)
  - saturation supporting signals: queue time, admission rejects (if available)

Acceptance criteria
- Metrics are present in the metrics backend for a canary Dedicated deployment in us-east and eu-west.
- For a controlled load test (fixed prompt/response lengths), derived throughput matches expected within +/- 5% over a 10 minute window.
- Streaming and non-streaming requests produce the same total output token counts for identical prompts/max_tokens settings (no double-counting).
- Metrics include the labels deployment_id, model, region on every sample.
- SRE can write an SLO query that returns a single time series per deployment_id (after aggregation) without additional joins.
- Add automated tests:
  - Unit test: streaming path increments output tokens exactly once per token
  - Integration test: one request, validate counters increment (prompt tokens, output tokens, generation time, requests_total)

Test plan (details)
- Local: run gateway with a fake streaming responder emitting N chunks; assert generation_tokens_total increases by exactly N_tokens, not 2*N.
- Staging: run k6 load against a Dedicated canary (single model pinned) and compare:
  - sum(output tokens) from logs/billing events vs counter delta
  - generation_time_seconds_total monotonic increase and reasonable per-request averages
- Regression: add a golden test for a known streaming payload that previously caused token double-counting.

Operational notes
- These metrics will be used for customer-facing reporting in the Console and for alerting. Treat any semantic change as a breaking change requiring review from SRE + Observability.
- Add a short docstring block in code referencing the Dedicated throughput SLO initiative and where to find the SLO definition / ADR.

Links
- Initiative ticket: PM-482 Dedicated throughput SLO and tier definitions
- Related engineering work: ENG-19288 burn-rate alerts, ENG-19257 tier-aware admission control
- Expected PR: PR-19321 add deployment throughput metrics

Open questions
- Do we want to include caching/batching effects explicitly (e.g., cache_hit label)? Current proposal: no; too high cardinality and confusing for SLO.
- Do we need to split “generated tokens” vs “delivered tokens” for streaming disconnects? Current proposal: requests_total will capture client_canceled, but output tokens counted are only delivered tokens. Confirm with SRE if SLO should treat client cancels as excluded.

Requested reviewers
- Aisha Bello (SRE implementation)
- Sergio Costa (Observability dashboards)
- Nadia Rahman (metering/cost attribution)

This ticket adds per-deployment throughput metrics with consistent token accounting semantics (esp. streaming) and the required labels.

Scope
- Emit canonical throughput “inputs” at the inference gateway / request path for all Dedicated deployments:
  - tokens counted (input + output) with explicit definitions
  - wall clock generation time used for throughput calculations
  - request counts by completion outcome
- All metrics MUST be tagged by:
  - deployment_id (Dedicated deployment identifier; required)
  - model (canonical model slug as used in routing/catalog)
  - region (Redwood region for the serving pool)
- Provide dashboard hooks (metric names/labels/query examples) for Observability to add panels.
- Add acceptance tests and backfill documentation for metric semantics so SRE can write the SLO definition without re-litigating token accounting.

Non-goals
- This ticket does not define the final SLO thresholds or tier entitlements (handled in PM-482 / SRE SLO doc).
